data.adaptation_notes: DEKA_LB is a long-duration maize cultivar recommended for low potential AEZs (LM3, LM4, LM5) in Embu county, Kenya. It is able to maintain yield levels under climate change scenarios, making it suitable for adaptation to increased temperatures. Optimal management includes early planting within the window, plant population of 40–50, and fertilizer nitrogen application of 60–80 kg/ha depending on AEZ and season. DSSAT and APSIM simulations indicate that early planting increases yields by 25% in SR season and 7% in LR season. High moisture stress is a major yield limiting factor in LM4 and LM5. Adaptation strategy for different agro-ecological zones with best combination of planting time, plant population, variety and fertilizer nitrogen for LR and SR seasons. Impact of climate change on the performance of different maize varieties under cultivation in different AEZs in Embu county of Kenya. This could be attributed to various factors such as differences in interpreting and translating farmer description of the resource endowment into model parameters, inability of the models to capture the effects of biotic stresses such as pests, diseases and weeds, inaccuracies in estimating per hectare yields from bags per plot as reported by farmers and inaccuracies in defining the initial conditions. This included, dates of sowing, days to tasselling and flowering, days to maturity and grain and dry matter yields at harvest.

This included, dates of sowing, days to tasselling and flowering, days to maturity and grain and dry matter yields at harvest.

data.adaptation_notes: DEKA_LB is a long-duration maize cultivar recommended for low potential AEZs (LM3, LM4, LM5) in Embu county, Kenya. It is able to maintain yield levels under climate change scenarios, making it suitable for adaptation to increased temperatures. Optimal management includes early planting within the window, plant population of 40–50, and fertilizer nitrogen application of 60–80 kg/ha depending on AEZ and season. DSSAT and APSIM simulations indicate that early planting increases yields by 25% in SR season and 7% in LR season. High moisture stress is a major yield limiting factor in LM4 and LM5. Adaptation strategy for different agro-ecological zones with best combination of planting time, plant population, variety and fertilizer nitrogen for LR and SR seasons. Impact of climate change on the performance of different maize varieties under cultivation in different AEZs in Embu county of Kenya. This could be attributed to various factors such as differences in interpreting and translating farmer description of the resource endowment into model parameters, inability of the models to capture the effects of biotic stresses such as pests, diseases and weeds, inaccuracies in estimating per hectare yields from bags per plot as reported by farmers and inaccuracies in defining the initial conditions. This included, dates of sowing, days to tasselling and flowering, days to maturity and grain and dry matter yields at harvest.

location_contexts.management_context: Farmer fields; experimental farm; adaptation strategies tested with DSSAT and APSIM
location_contexts.evidence: The maize varieties H511, H513 and Katumani were selected to represent the cultivars used by farmers in the Embu county. APSIM and DSSAT crop models were calibrated for these maize varieties using the experimental data as discussed in the material and methods section. The observed crop phenology, biomass and yield are satisfactorily simulated by the models for three cultivars at Embu.

location_contexts.location_name: AEZs UM2, UM3, LM3, LM4, LM5
location_contexts.management_context: Simulated yields under baseline and climate change scenarios; adaptation strategies tested
location_contexts.evidence: Impact of climate change on maize production in the five AEZs is assessed. Maize yields varied in response to differences in the magnitude of projected change in surface temperatures and rainfall by different GCMs under different scenarios. Maize yields also varied depending on the AEZ, season, and management practices.

location_contexts.season: SR season of 2000; LR and SR seasons of 2001
location_contexts.management_context: Experimental data used for calibration and validation of DSSAT and APSIM for Katumani
location_contexts.evidence: The experiment included all these varieties and was conducted over three seasons i.e., SR season of 2000 and LR and SR seasons of 2001. All the available data was compiled from the research reports as well as personnel communication with the researcher concerned.

coefficients.notes: Extracted from a paper-reported DSSAT coefficient table.

location_contexts.management_context: LR: 15–30 Mar, 50,000 plants/ha, H513, 70–80 kg N/ha; SR: 1–15 Oct (UM3), 1–15 Nov (UM2), 40,000 plants/ha, H513, 60–70 kg N/ha
location_contexts.evidence: Pioneer is mapped to H513 in DSSAT simulations. Adaptation strategies recommend H513 for LR season in UM2, UM3, LM3 AEZs, with optimal planting time mid-March and plant population 50,000/ha. In SR season, H513 is recommended for UM3. DSSAT simulations show higher yield increases under climate change in high potential AEZs (UM2, UM3, LM3) and negative impacts in low potential AEZs (LM4, LM5).

data.adaptation_notes: Pioneer is mapped to H513 in DSSAT simulations, representing long-duration, high-yield maize. Adaptation strategies recommend H513 for LR season in UM2, UM3, LM3 AEZs, with optimal planting time mid-March and plant population 50,000/ha. In SR season, H513 is recommended for UM3. DSSAT simulations show higher yield increases under climate change in high potential AEZs (UM2, UM3, LM3) and negative impacts in low potential AEZs (LM4, LM5). Early planting within the window improves yield. Fertilizer nitrogen up to 80 kg/ha increases yield in SR season. High moisture stress is a major yield limiting factor in LM4 and LM5. 3.2.7 Impacts of soil fertility and nitrogen application. Probability density function of projections in maximum temperature by GCMs at Embu (left) and Ishiara (right) in Embu county, Kenya (red dotted line denotes observed temperature). This could be attributed to various factors such as differences in interpreting and translating farmer description of the resource endowment into model parameters, inability of the models to capture the effects of biotic stresses such as pests, diseases and weeds, inaccuracies in estimating per hectare yields from bags per plot as reported by farmers and inaccuracies in defining the initial conditions. Observed (average of three seasons) and DSSAT and APSIM modeled phenology and grain and biomass yields of three maize varieties.
